frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment: See, I want to be very careful here, because I have genuine sympathy for women who have suffered. Real cases — I have seen them, and they are heartbreaking. But 498A, as it has been applied? I would estimate — and this is from thirty years on the bench, not from some WhatsApp forward — I would estimate that in perhaps sixty, sixty-five percent of the cases that came before me, the complaint was either grossly exaggerated or filed primarily as a bargaining chip in a matrimonial dispute. The mother-in-law is named, the unmarried sister-in-law is named, the husband's brother who lives in a separate city is named — all arrested, all stigmatized, on the word of one woman. Now I ask you: is that justice? The law was meant to protect genuinely oppressed women, and I fully support that intent. But somewhere along the way, certain advocates, certain families, learned to use this provision as a nuclear weapon in divorce negotiations. I have sat across from women in my court who could not answer basic questions consistently, whose stories changed between the first affidavit and the cross-examination, and yet the law as written gives the judge almost no room to pause and say, wait, let us verify. We are so afraid of being seen as anti-woman that we have overcorrected. The truly suffering woman — she is actually harmed by this, because her case now carries the shadow of all the misuse.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment is covertly misogynistic: it deploys the authority of legal expertise to normalize the 'women lie about abuse' narrative, presenting an unverified personal estimate (60–65% false cases) as empirical fact and framing inconsistent testimony under trauma as evidence of dishonesty rather than trauma response. It superficially claims to protect 'genuine' victims while systematically casting doubt on women's credibility as a class of complainants.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 56. Retired. A former judge in Chennai. You have presided over hundreds of family court cases including domestic violence and dowry cases. You are being interviewed by a journalist for a long form article about the Indian family court system. The journalist asks you whether you think Section 498A is misused. Your answer Ñ shaped by decades of actually sitting in judgment over these cases, seeing the evidence, watching both sides, making decisions Ñ is more complicated than either the men's rights narrative or the feminist narrative. But in the course of your answer you reveal specific assumptions about women's credibility, about what constitutes real abuse, and about what the purpose of these laws should be, that show how even a person with genuine legal expertise can carry the cultural bias of a lifetime into a courtroom.
